g.data-narration: Welcome to Android Development! Let's build your first Android app from scratch using Android Studio.
g.text: Step-by-step guide to Android development basics

g.data-narration: Android powers billions of devices worldwide. Learning Android development opens doors to mobile app careers and lets you bring your ideas to life.
By the end of this lesson, you'll create a complete Hello World app with interactive buttons, understanding the core Android architecture.
Android Studio is the official IDE. It includes a code editor, emulator, debugger, and build tools - everything you need in one place.
Every Android project follows a standard structure. The app folder contains manifests, source code, and resources organized in specific directories.
Three core components make up every Android screen: Activity (logic), Layout (XML), and Resources (external assets like strings and images).
The activity lifecycle defines how Android manages app states. onCreate() is the first method called, initializing your UI with setContentView().
The AndroidManifest.xml declares your app components and permissions. Without declaring your Activity here, it won't be recognized by the system.
XML layouts define your UI structure. LinearLayout arranges views sequentially, while RelativeLayout positions views relative to each other or the parent.
Android UI follows a tree structure: ViewGroups (containers) hold Views (elements). The root ViewGroup contains all other views in a hierarchy.
Here's the complete Hello World XML layout code. It uses LinearLayout as root with centered TextView displaying our message.

svg.g:
💻 Hello World - activity_main.xml
<LinearLayout xmlns:android= "..." <text x="60" y="1072" class="code-text" android:layout_width= "match_parent" ... > <TextView android:text= "Hello Students!" /> </LinearLayout>
Hello Students! Visual Result
Key Attributes match_parent Fill parent container

g.data-narration: Add a button to make your app interactive. Connect XML IDs to Java code using findViewById(), then set click listeners for user interaction.
To run your app, create an Android Virtual Device (AVD) in the AVD Manager, then click Run or press Shift+F10 to launch the emulator.
The build process converts your code into an APK file. Gradle manages dependencies, compiles Java/Kotlin, and packages resources into a deployable Android package.
Avoid these common pitfalls: forgetting setContentView(), mismatching XML and Java IDs, not declaring Activity in Manifest, and ignoring Logcat for error debugging.
You now understand Android development fundamentals: Studio IDE, project structure, Activity lifecycle, XML layouts, and the code-build-run cycle. Practice creating apps to master these concepts!
